old_claim.note: Philosophy is contextual infrastructure or shared knowledge, not a strict hard prerequisite.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency is retained because early written legal systems are anchored to Sumerian law-code evidence, not philosophical traditions.

source_shape.source_locator: Schoyen Collection entry anchors the Ur-Nammu law code around 2100 BCE in Sumerian cuneiform.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The earliest surviving written-law evidence is Sumerian and predates the graph philosophy node.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Philosophy can influence later jurisprudence, but it is not a prerequisite for early written law-code collections.

ontology_before: Early Legal Systems treated Philosophy as a contextual prerequisite.
ontology_after: Early Legal Systems is scoped to written law-code evidence and keeps writing/record-keeping dependencies only.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Invariant changed: Early Legal Systems must not directly depend on Philosophy unless narrowed to legal philosophy or jurisprudence.
would_reject_if: The node is re-scoped to legal philosophy rather than written legal systems.
A source shows the Ur-Nammu law-code evidence should not be used for early legal-system chronology.
The philosophy node is re-scoped to a much earlier legal-abstraction tradition with direct evidence.
short_reason: Early written law codes predate the philosophy node.
